title: Backtracking
description: Fully explaining the 'try, recurse, undo' pattern previewed with subsets in Recursion in DSA -- systematically exploring choices and abandoning ones that fail.
objectives: Understand backtracking as choose -> explore -> unchoose
Generate all permutations of a small array
Solve a constrained problem (combinations that sum to a target) by pruning invalid paths early

sections.text: In Recursion in DSA, generateSubsets tried each choice, recursed, then UNDID that choice with current.remove(...) before trying the next one. That pattern is backtracking, and today we go deeper.

A systematic way of exploring all possible choices for a problem by making a choice, recursively exploring its consequences, and then undoing (backtracking) that choice to try the next alternative -- often abandoning a path early once it's known to be invalid.

sections.title: Choose -> Explore -> Unchoose
points: CHOOSE: add a value to the current partial solution
EXPLORE: recurse deeper with that choice in place
UNCHOOSE: remove the value again, restoring state, before trying the next option

sections.text: used[] tracks which elements are already part of the current permutation, so we don't reuse one twice in the same arrangement. Every permutation of 3 elements gives 3! = 6 total arrangements, matching result.size().
Backtracking's real power shows up when we can DETECT a path is doomed before fully exploring it, and skip it immediately -- this is called pruning.

if (target < 0) { return; } is the PRUNE step -- the instant a partial sum overshoots the target, there is NO way adding more positive candidates fixes it, so we stop exploring that branch immediately rather than continuing pointlessly. Without pruning, backtracking still works, but explores far more dead-end paths than necessary.

The loop starts at i = start (not 0), so once index i is chosen, later recursive calls can only pick from index i onward -- this is what keeps each combination in non-decreasing index order and avoids producing both [2,2,3] and [3,2,2] as separate results. current.add(candidates[i]) followed by recursing with i (not i + 1) is what allows the SAME candidate to be reused (like the two 2s in [2,2,3]).
5. Why Not Just Brute Force Every Possibility Blindly?

sections.title: Backtracking vs pure brute force
points: Pure brute force would generate EVERY possible combination first, THEN filter out invalid ones -- wasted work on paths that were doomed from the start
Backtracking checks validity AS IT GOES, abandoning a path (pruning) the moment it becomes impossible, rather than finishing it uselessly
This makes backtracking dramatically faster in practice, even though its worst-case complexity can still be exponential for genuinely hard problems

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the UNCHOOSE step (current.remove(...), used[i] = false) -- without it, state leaks between branches and results become incorrect.
Forgetting to add a PRUNE condition when one is available, exploring hopeless paths that waste time without changing the final answer.
Confusing backtracking with plain recursion -- backtracking specifically involves undoing a choice to explore alternatives, not just recursing once per call.

Adding the SAME reference (like current itself) to the result list instead of a COPY (new ArrayList<>(current)) -- since current keeps changing, the result would end up holding stale, incorrect, or all-identical references.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is backtracking?
questions.answer: A systematic technique for exploring all possible choices for a problem: make a choice, recursively explore its consequences, then undo (backtrack) that choice to try the next alternative -- often abandoning invalid paths early.

questions.question: What are the three steps of the backtracking pattern?
questions.answer: Choose (make a tentative choice), Explore (recurse deeper with that choice in place), and Unchoose (undo the choice, restoring state, before trying the next option).

questions.question: What is pruning, and why does it matter?
questions.answer: Pruning means detecting that a partial solution can never lead to a valid answer, and abandoning that branch immediately instead of continuing to explore it uselessly -- this can dramatically reduce the actual work done, even though worst-case complexity can remain exponential.

questions.question: Why must a COPY of the current partial solution be added to the results list, not the list itself?
questions.answer: Because current keeps being mutated (added to and removed from) as the recursion continues -- adding the same reference would mean every entry in the results list ends up reflecting current's FINAL state, not the state at the moment each solution was found.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Backtracking systematically explores all possible choices for a problem using a choose-explore-unchoose pattern: make a tentative choice, recurse deeper with it in place, then undo it to try the next alternative -- exactly what generateSubsets previewed in Recursion in DSA. Pruning strengthens this by detecting a partial solution can never succeed (like a running sum already exceeding the target) and abandoning that branch immediately, avoiding wasted exploration. A common bug is adding the live, mutating current list directly to a results list instead of a copy, since current changes throughout the recursion. Backtracking underlies permutation generation, combination-sum problems, and is the foundation for the Backtracking-tagged LeetCode problems covered later in this course.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Backtracking = exploring a maze by trying a path, and the MOMENT you hit a dead end, walking back to the last fork and trying a different direction -- never starting the whole maze over from scratch. Choose. Explore. Unchoose. Repeat.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What are the three steps of the backtracking pattern?
What does 'pruning' mean in backtracking, and why is it useful?
Why must you add a copy of the current list to the results, not the list itself?
How many permutations exist for an array of n distinct elements?

sections.title: Subsets
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums of unique elements, return all possible subsets (the power set). The solution set must not contain duplicate subsets.
hints: At every recursive call, first record the CURRENT partial subset as a valid answer, then try extending it with each remaining element from a growing start index.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Use bitmasking: each of the 2^n numbers from 0 to 2^n-1 represents one subset, where bit i decides whether nums[i] is included.
approaches.output: For nums = [1,2,3]: 8 subsets total, including [] and [1,2,3]

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Backtracking: record the current subset at every call, then extend it with each remaining element starting from a growing index.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n * 2^n) for the result, O(n) recursion depth

sections.title: Permutations
sections.problemStatement: Given an array nums of distinct integers, return all possible permutations, in any order.
hints: This is exactly this lesson's permute() function -- choose an unused element, recurse, then unchoose it (mark unused again).

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Same backtracking shape is really the standard approach here -- there isn't a meaningfully different 'brute force' beyond generating and checking every arrangement, which backtracking already does minimally.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Identical backtracking approach -- for permutations, this IS the standard efficient solution, since n! permutations must all be produced regardless.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) recursion depth beyond the result

sections.title: Combinations
sections.problemStatement: Given two integers n and k, return all possible combinations of k numbers chosen from the range [1, n].
hints: Same backtracking shape as Subsets, but only record the current combination once it reaches exactly length k, instead of at every call.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Generate every subset of [1..n] via bitmasking, then keep only those of size k.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Backtracking with a growing start index, recording the combination only when it reaches length k.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(k) recursion depth beyond the result

sections.title: Combination Sum
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of distinct integers candidates and a target integer target, return all unique combinations where the chosen numbers sum to target. The same number may be chosen an unlimited number of times.
hints: This is exactly the combinationSum function already built in this lesson's Section 4, including the pruning step for when target goes negative.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Backtracking WITHOUT the target < 0 pruning -- still correct, but explores many more dead-end branches before failing.
approaches.timeComplexity: Exponential in the worst case, without early pruning

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Backtracking with explicit target < 0 pruning, exactly as built in this lesson.
approaches.timeComplexity: Exponential worst case, but with pruning explores far fewer branches in practice

sections.title: Letter Combinations of a Phone Number
sections.problemStatement: Given a string containing digits from 2-9, return all possible letter combinations the number could represent, based on the standard telephone keypad mapping.
constraints: digits[i] is a digit in the range ['2', '9']
hints: For each digit, CHOOSE one of its mapped letters, recurse into the next digit, then UNCHOOSE (implicitly, by not appending) to try the next letter.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Iteratively build up the result list, expanding it by every possible letter of each new digit -- correct, but less naturally recursive.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Backtracking: for each digit position, try every mapped letter, recurse to the next digit, then backtrack.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) recursion depth beyond the result

sections.title: Generate Parentheses
sections.problemStatement: Given n pairs of parentheses, generate all combinations of well-formed parentheses.
hints: Track the count of open and close parentheses used so far. You can add '(' if openCount < n; you can add ')' only if closeCount < openCount (a valid prefix never has more closes than opens) -- this pruning is what keeps every generated string well-formed.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Generate ALL possible 2n-length strings of '(' and ')', then check each for validity using a stack-based check.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Backtracking that only ever builds VALID prefixes, tracking open/close counts directly, so no invalid strings are ever generated or checked afterward.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(4^n / sqrt(n)) -- bounded by the nth Catalan number
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) recursion depth beyond the result

sections.title: Word Search
sections.problemStatement: Given an m x n grid of characters board and a string word, return true if word exists in the grid, formed by adjacent cells (horizontally or vertically), without reusing the same cell twice.
hints: From each matching cell, backtrack into its 4 neighbors, temporarily marking the current cell as visited (e.g. overwrite it) so the path can't reuse it, then restore it when backtracking.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Try starting the search from EVERY cell, using a separate visited boolean grid instead of marking the board directly.
approaches.output: For the given board and word = "ABCCED": true
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(m * n) for the visited grid

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Same backtracking, but mark visited cells by temporarily overwriting the board itself (e.g. with a sentinel character), avoiding a separate visited grid.
approaches.output: For the given board and word = "ABCCED": true
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(L) recursion depth, no extra grid needed

sections.title: N-Queens
sections.problemStatement: The n-queens puzzle is placing n queens on an n x n chessboard so that no two queens attack each other. Return all distinct board configurations, each represented as a list of strings using 'Q' and '.'.

examples.explanation: 2 distinct solutions exist for the 4-queens puzzle.

hints: Place one queen per ROW, choosing its column via backtracking -- track which columns and both diagonals are already under attack to prune invalid placements immediately.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Try every possible column for the current row, checking the FULL board for conflicts each time (rather than tracking attacked columns/diagonals incrementally).
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n!) roughly, with an O(row) safety check per placement
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the queens array plus the result

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Track attacked columns and both diagonals with boolean sets, giving O(1) safety checks instead of rescanning previous rows each time.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n!) roughly, with O(1) safety checks per placement
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the tracking arrays plus the result

sections.title: Sudoku Solver
sections.problemStatement: Write a program to solve a Sudoku puzzle by filling the empty cells, following the standard Sudoku rules: each row, column, and 3x3 sub-box must contain the digits 1-9 without repetition. The board is modified in place.

examples.input: A partially filled 9x9 board with '.' marking empty cells
examples.output: The same board, fully solved in place (too large to display cell-by-cell here -- see the official LeetCode example).

constraints: The given board has exactly one solution
hints: For each empty cell, try digits 1-9; a digit is valid if it doesn't already appear in the same row, column, or 3x3 box.

This is a much larger backtracking search space than N-Queens, but the same choose/explore/unchoose skeleton applies -- place a digit, recurse to the next empty cell, and if no digit leads to a solution, undo and try the next candidate.

approaches.label: Brute Force (conceptually -- still requires backtracking)
approaches.explanation: There is no meaningfully simpler brute force for Sudoku beyond backtracking itself -- exhaustively trying every possible full board assignment without pruning is computationally infeasible, so even the 'naive' solution must backtrack.
approaches.output: Board is solved in place -- no return value needed
approaches.timeComplexity: Exponential in the worst case (bounded by 9^(number of empty cells))
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space (beyond the recursion stack) -- the board itself is modified in place

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Same backtracking skeleton -- this problem doesn't have a fundamentally faster general algorithm, so the optimization is in the isValid check's efficiency and pruning as early as possible, exactly as shown.
approaches.output: Board is solved in place -- no return value needed
approaches.timeComplexity: Exponential in the worst case, but the constant-size 9x9 board and early pruning make it fast in practice
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space beyond the recursion stack
